mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW X5 3.0sd / xDrive35d engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned X5 3.0sd/xDrive35d engine typically costs £2,500–£5,500 supply only depending on generation. Fitting adds £2,000–£3,000 depending on location and workshop rates. A used N57 engine costs between £4,000 and £5,500 with warranty. Main dealer new engines start from £9,000–£13,000+ supply only. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW X5 3.0sd / xDrive35d engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends entirely on which engine generation your X5 has. The M57TU2D30 (2008–2010 3.0sd) is widely regarded as one of BMW's most durable diesels — many owners report 300,000+ kilometres without major issues. The N57 (2010–2018 xDrive35d) brought refinement but has more documented timing chain and turbo issues. N57 reliability is rated at approximately 4 out of 5 stars. Check the Era Map above for your specific car.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW X5 3.0sd / xDrive35d?

acceptedAnswer.text: Timing chain failure on N57 engines (2010–2018) — can occur without warning and destroy the engine, turbocharger and vacuum system failures — the biturbo setup has failures much more frequently than single-turbo variants, EGR cooler failures (subject to BMW recall 11E-A03), and DPF clogging from short urban journeys.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW X5 3.0sd / xDrive35d engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and condition. Against a well-kept £8,000–£18,000 X5, a £3,500–£5,500 recon is 20–70% of the car's value. For a clean E70 M57 example, replacement is almost always worth it given the engine's legendary durability. For F15 N57 examples with no chain history, evaluate the car's overall condition before committing.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW X5 3.0sd / xDrive35d engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. As a rule of thumb: E70 3.0sd (2008–2010) has M57TU2D30, E70 LCI xDrive35d (2010–2013) has N57D30, F15 xDrive35d (2013–2018) has N57D30A. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the N57 timing chain, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — if caught early. A preventative chain replacement (£1,500–£3,000) is far cheaper than a catastrophic chain snap (£5,000–£8,000 engine replacement). At the first sign of cold-start rattle, book an inspection immediately. BMW claims the timing chain is a 'lifetime part' and does not service it, but owners have reported failures with no warning. Sticking to 10,000-mile oil service intervals can help minimise wear.

eraMap.title: BMW X5 3.0sd / xDrive35d — Which Engine Does Your Car Actually Have?

rows.eraNote: 3.0L twin-turbo — 272 hp, 565 Nm. Robust M57 family, swirl flap and turbo issues documented
3.0L twin-turbo — 306 hp, 600 Nm. More refined than M57, timing chain and turbo concerns
3.0L twin-turbo — 258–309 hp, 560–630 Nm. N57TU with improved emissions, similar risk profile

problems.title: Timing Chain Failure (N57 Engines, E70 LCI & F15)
problems.symptoms: Rattling or slapping noise from the engine on start-up, poor engine performance, loss of power, acceleration problems, metallic ticking
problems.typicalMileage: 90,000–120,000 miles — oil dilution from short drives and DPF regens can accelerate wear

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattle, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: N57 timing chain failure is a well-documented issue. One X5 35d owner reported: "The car would not crank. Towed to dealership and they said the timing chain had broken and the engine was gone. No warning light, codes, nothing!" BMW claims the timing chain is a "lifetime part" and does not service it, yet failures are common. At the first sign of cold-start rattling, get the chain inspected immediately. A £1,500–£3,000 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than a £5,000–£8,000 engine replacement.

failureLink.label: Read the full timing chain failure guide

problems.title: Turbocharger & Vacuum System Failure (All Generations)
problems.symptoms: Loss of power, excessive smoke from exhaust, unusual whistling or siren-like noises, drivetrain malfunction warning
problems.typicalMileage: 60,000–120,000 miles — vacuum solenoids and turbo actuators are common failure points
problems.repairCost: £800–£2,500 (turbocharger rebuild or replacement; vacuum solenoid replacement)

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if you hear whistling or experience power loss

problems.recommendation: The 35d's biturbo setup has failures much more frequently than single-turbo variants. Common issues include vacuum solenoids (pressure converters) for the various flaps controlling the biturbo system — the turbine control flap, bypass plate and wastegate are all actuated via vacuum. EGR flow insufficient (P0401) and turbo underboost (P0299) codes are frequently reported on X5 35d models. If you experience these symptoms, have the turbo system and vacuum lines inspected immediately.

failureLink.label: Read the full turbo failure guide

problems.title: EGR Cooler & DPF Issues (All Generations)
problems.symptoms: White smoke from exhaust, coolant loss, drivetrain malfunction warning, DPF warning light, poor fuel economy
problems.typicalMileage: Any mileage — short urban journeys accelerate the issue
problems.repairCost: £300–£800 (EGR cleaning / DPF regeneration) / £1,000–£2,500 (EGR cooler or DPF replacement)
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (does not progress to engine replacement if addressed)

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: verify EGR recall completion status; coolant loss can cause engine damage

problems.recommendation: EGR cooler failure is a well-documented issue on X5 35d models. One owner reported: "This post is to add to the general knowledge base concerning 35d reliability and the EGR cooler recall". BMW issued recall 11E-A03 for check/replacement of the high-pressure EGR valve on X5 xDrive35d (E70) models. Thermostat failure can prevent DPF regeneration, leading to DPF clogging. Verify recall completion before buying any X5 35d — this is a free fix at the dealership.

failureLink.label: Read the full EGR cooler failure guide

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: X5 3.0sd / xDrive35d — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Preventative — catches the fault before catastrophic failure
Piston-valve contact means full replacement required
Catch early to prevent secondary engine damage
Free under recall; straightforward replacement out of warranty

buyingChecks.title: Buying This X5 3.0sd / xDrive35d? Check These First
buyIf: * E70 M57-engined car (2008–2010) — these are the most durable X5 diesels
* Documented timing chain history on any N57-engined car (2010–2018)
* Full service history with BMW Longlife-04 oil specification and 10,000-mile oil changes
avoidIf: * N57-engined car with cold-start rattle and no chain history — this is a ticking time bomb
* No proof of EGR recall work — this is a free fix; lack of it suggests neglect
* Poor service history — diesel BMWs are unforgiving of neglect
* DPF warning lights or drivetrain malfunction warnings with no diagnosis

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The X5 3.0sd and xDrive35d badges span two very different engine generations. The M57TU2D30 (2008–2010 3.0sd) is widely regarded as one of BMW's most durable diesels — "the M57 is a robust and versatile diesel engine" with many owners reporting 300,000+ kilometres without major issues. The N57 (2010–2018 xDrive35d) brought refinement but has more documented timing chain and turbo issues. Buy a 2008–2010 M57 if you want the most durable option. Buy a well-maintained N57 with documented chain history if you want newer refinement. Buy on documented history, not on price alone.

cta.label: → Compare X5 3.0sd / xDrive35d engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW X5 3.0sd or xDrive35d (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling or slapping noise (timing chain warning — this is critical)
* Turbo Health — Listen for whistling or siren-like noises under acceleration; check for power loss and drivetrain warnings
* Service History — Including which engine era (M57TU2D30 or N57) and confirmed oil-interval discipline
* EGR Recall — Verify EGR cooler recall completion (recall 11E-A03)
* DPF Health — Check for DPF warning lights; short journeys are a red flag
* Vacuum System — Any drivetrain warnings or performance issues? Vacuum solenoids are common failures
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Look for P0401 (EGR flow), P0299 (turbo underboost)
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures
